records.ety: [OE. wonder, wunder, AS. wundor; akin to D. wonder, OS. wundar, OHG. wuntar, G. wunder, Icel. undr, Sw. & Dan. under, and perhaps to Gr. <?/ to gaze at.]

defs: That emotion which is excited by novelty, or the presentation to the sight or mind of something new, unusual, strange, great, extraordinary, or not well understood; surprise; astonishment; admiration; amazement.
quotes: They were filled with wonder and amazement at that which had happened unto him.
Wonder is the effect of novelty upon ignorance.
notes: ☞ Wonder expresses less than astonishment, and much less than amazement. It differs from admiration, as now used, in not being necessarily accompanied with love, esteem, or approbation.

defs: A cause of wonder; that which excites surprise; a strange thing; a prodigy; a miracle.
quotes: To try things oft, and never to give over, doth wonders.
I am as a wonder unto many.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Wondered (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Wondering.]

defs: To be affected with surprise or admiration; to be struck with astonishment; to be amazed; to marvel.
quotes: I could not sufficiently wonder at the intrepidity of these diminutive mortals.
We cease to wonder at what we understand.

defs: To feel doubt and curiosity; to wait with uncertain expectation; to query in the mind; as, he wondered why they came.
quotes: I wonder, in my soul, What you would ask me, that I should deny.
After that he said a wonder thing.
